an2.32:1.1: “Monks, I will teach you the level of a person of no integrity and the level of a person of integrity.
an2.32:1.2: Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
an2.32:1.3: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to him.
an2.32:2.1: “Now what is the level of a person of no integrity?
an2.32:2.2: A person of no integrity is ungrateful & unthankful.
an2.32:2.3: This ingratitude, this lack of thankfulness, is advocated by rude people.
an2.32:2.4: It is entirely on the level of people of no integrity.
an2.32:2.5: A person of integrity is grateful & thankful.
an2.32:2.6: This gratitude, this thankfulness, is advocated by civil people.
an2.32:2.7: It is entirely on the level of people of integrity.”
an2.33:1.1: “I tell you, monks, there are two people who are not easy to repay.
an2.33:1.4: Even if you were to carry your mother on one shoulder & your father on the other shoulder for 100 years, and were to look after them by anointing, massaging, bathing, & rubbing their limbs,
an2.33:1.5: and they were to defecate & urinate right there [on your shoulders], you would not in that way pay or repay your parents.
an2.33:1.6: If you were to establish your mother & father in absolute sovereignty over this great earth, abounding in the seven treasures, you would not in that way pay or repay your parents.
an2.33:1.8: Mother & father do much for their children. They care for them, they nourish them, they introduce them to this world.
an2.33:1.9: But anyone who rouses his unbelieving mother & father, settles & establishes them in conviction; rouses his unvirtuous mother & father, settles & establishes them in virtue; rouses his stingy mother & father, settles & establishes them in generosity; rouses his foolish mother & father, settles & establishes them in discernment: To this extent one pays & repays one’s mother & father.“
an2.36:1.2: On one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
an2.36:1.3: And on that occasion Ven. Sāriputta was staying near Sāvatthī in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother.
an2.36:1.6: “Yes, friend,” the monks responded to him.
an2.36:1.8: “Friends, I will teach you the person fettered outside & the person fettered inside.
an2.36:1.9: Listen to that & pay careful attention. I will speak.”
an2.36:1.10: “As you say, friend,” the monks responded to him.
an2.36:2.1: “And which, friends, is the person fettered inside?
an2.36:2.2: There is the case where a monk is virtuous. He dwells restrained in accordance with the Pāṭimokkha, consummate in his behavior & sphere of activity. He trains himself, having undertaken the training rules, seeing danger in the slightest faults.
an2.36:2.3: He—with the breakup of the body, after death—reappears in a certain deva-host.
an2.36:2.4: Falling from there, he is a returner, one who returns to this state of being.
an2.36:2.5: This is called a person fettered inside, a returner, one who returns to this state of being.
an2.36:3.1: “And which is the person fettered outside?
an2.36:3.2: There is the case where a monk is virtuous. He dwells restrained in accordance with the Pāṭimokkha, consummate in his behavior & sphere of activity. He trains himself, having undertaken the training rules, seeing danger in the slightest faults.
an2.36:3.3: He enters & remains in a certain peaceful awareness release.
an2.36:3.4: He—with the breakup of the body, after death—reappears in a certain deva-host.
an2.36:3.5: Falling from there, he is a non-returner, one who doesn’t return to this state of being.
an2.36:3.6: This is called a person fettered outside, a non-returner, one who doesn’t return to this state of being.
an2.36:4.1: “Then again, a monk is virtuous. …  seeing danger in the slightest faults.
an2.36:4.2: He is one who practices for disenchantment, for dispassion, for & the cessation of sensuality.
an2.36:4.3: He is one who practices for disenchantment, for dispassion, for & the cessation of becoming.
an2.36:4.4: He is one who practices for the ending of craving.
an2.36:4.5: He is one who practices for the ending of greed.
an2.36:4.6: He—with the breakup of the body, after death—reappears in a certain deva-host.
an2.36:4.7: Falling from there, he is a non-returner, one who doesn’t return to this state of being.
an2.36:4.8: This is called a person fettered outside, a non-returner, one who doesn’t return to this state of being.”
an2.36:5.1: Then many Like-minded Devas went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, stood to one side. As they were standing there, they said to him,
an2.36:5.2: “Lord, Ven. Sāriputta, in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother, is teaching the monks about the person fettered inside & the person fettered outside.
an2.36:5.4: It would be good, lord, if the Blessed One went to Ven. Sāriputta, out of sympathy.”
an2.36:5.5: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
an2.36:5.6: Then—just as a strong man might extend his flexed arm or flex his extended arm—the Blessed One disappeared from Jeta’s Grove and re-appeared in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother, right in front of Ven. Sāriputta.
an2.36:5.7: He sat down on a seat laid out.
an2.36:5.8: Ven. Sāriputta, bowing down to the Blessed One, sat to one side.
an2.36:5.9: As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him,
an2.36:6.1: “Just now, Sāriputta, many Like-minded Devas went to me and, on arrival, having bowed down to me, stood to one side. As they were standing there, they said to me,
an2.36:6.2: ‘Lord, Ven. Sāriputta, in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother, is teaching the monks about the person fettered inside & the person fettered outside.
an2.36:6.4: It would be good, lord, if the Blessed One went to Ven. Sāriputta, out of sympathy.’
an2.36:6.5: “Those devas, Sāriputta— whether there are ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or sixty—can stand on an area the size of a hole pierced by the point of an awl and not disturb one another.
an2.36:6.6: And if the thought should occur to you,
an2.36:6.7: ‘Surely it was there [in their heaven] that those devas developed such a mind-state that—whether there are ten, twenty, thirty, forty, fifty, or sixty—they can stand on an area the size of a hole pierced by the point of an awl and not disturb one another,’
an2.36:6.8: it shouldn’t be seen that way.
an2.36:6.9: It was right here that they developed such a mind-state that—whether there are ten … and not disturb one another.
an2.36:6.11: ‘We will be peaceful in our faculties & peaceful in our hearts.’
an2.36:6.12: That’s how you should train yourselves.
an2.36:6.13: When you are peaceful in your faculties & peaceful in your hearts, peaceful will be your bodily actions, peaceful your verbal actions, peaceful your mental actions.
an2.36:6.14: [Thinking,] ‘We will render only peaceful service to our fellows in the holy life’:
an2.36:6.15: That’s how you should train yourselves.
an2.36:6.16: “The wanderers of other sects are lost, Sāriputta, in that they don’t get to hear this Dhamma sequence.”
an2.37:1.2: On one occasion Ven. Mahā Kaccāna was staying at Varaṇā on the shore of Kaddama (Muddy) Lake.
an2.37:1.3: Then Ārāmadaṇḍa the brahman went to Ven. Mahā Kaccāna and, on arrival, exchanged courteous greetings with him.
an2.37:1.4: After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to Ven. Mahā Kaccāna,
an2.37:1.5: “What is the cause, Master Kaccāna, what is the reason, why noble warriors dispute with noble warriors, brahmans dispute with brahmans, and householders dispute with householders?”
an2.37:1.6: “Brahman, it’s with relishing, possession, greed, bondage, & entrenchment in passion for sensuality as a cause that noble warriors dispute with noble warriors, brahmans dispute with brahmans, and householders dispute with householders.”
an2.37:2.1: “And what is the cause, Master Kaccāna, what is the reason, why contemplatives dispute with contemplatives?”
an2.37:2.2: “Brahman, it’s with relishing, possession, greed, bondage, & entrenchment in passion for views as a cause that contemplatives dispute with contemplatives.”
an2.37:3.1: “And is there anyone in the world, Master Kaccāna, who has overcome both this relishing… entrenchment in passion for sensuality, and… this relishing… entrenchment in passion for views?”
an2.37:3.2: “There is, brahman, someone in the world who has overcome both this relishing… entrenchment in passion for sensuality, and… this relishing… entrenchment in passion for views.”
an2.37:4.1: “And who in the world, Master Kaccāna, has overcome both this relishing… entrenchment in passion for sensuality, and… this relishing… entrenchment in passion for views?”
an2.37:4.2: “There is, brahman, in the eastern countryside, a city named Sāvatthī.
an2.37:4.3: There the Blessed One—the Worthy One, the Rightly Self-awakened One—is now dwelling.
an2.37:4.4: That Blessed One has overcome this relishing… entrenchment in passion for sensuality, and… this relishing… entrenchment in passion for views.”
an2.37:5.1: When this was said, Ārāmadaṇḍa the brahman—getting up from his seat, arranging his robe over one shoulder, lowering his right knee to the ground, and raising his hands palm-to-palm over the heart—exclaimed three times:
an2.37:6.1: “Homage to the Blessed One, the Worthy One, the Rightly Self-awakened One!
an2.37:7.1: Homage to the Blessed One, the Worthy One, the Rightly Self-awakened One!
an2.37:8.1: Homage to the Blessed One, the Worthy One, the Rightly Self-awakened One!
an2.37:9.1: For that Blessed One is one who has overcome both this relishing, possession, greed, bondage, & entrenchment in passion for sensuality, and this relishing, possession, greed, bondage, & entrenchment in passion for views!
an2.37:10.2: Just as if he were to place upright what was overturned, to reveal what was hidden, to show the way to one who was lost, or to carry a lamp into the dark so that those with eyes could see forms, in the same way has Master Kaccāna—through many lines of reasoning—made the Dhamma clear.
an2.37:10.3: I go to Master Gotama for refuge, to the Dhamma, & to the Saṅgha of monks.
an2.37:10.4: May Master Kaccāna remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forward, for life.”
an2.38:1.1: On one occasion Ven. Mahā Kaccāna was staying near Madhura in the Gundā Forest.
an2.38:1.2: Then Kaṇḍarāyana the brahman went to Ven. Mahā Kaccāna and on arrival exchanged courteous greetings with him. After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to Ven. Mahā Kaccāna,
an2.38:1.3: “I have heard it said, Master Kaccāna, that, ‘Kaccāna the contemplative does not raise his hands in respect to aged, venerable brahmans—advanced in years, come to the last stage of life—nor does he rise up to greet them, nor does he offer them a seat.’
an2.38:1.5: you don’t raise your hands in respect to aged, venerable brahmans—advanced in years, come to the last stage of life—nor rise up to greet them, nor offer them a seat,
an2.38:1.6: that is simply not right, Master Kaccāna.”
an2.38:2.1: “Brahman, the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, worthy & rightly self-awakened—has declared the level of one who is venerable and the level of one who is a youngster.
an2.38:2.2: Even if one is venerable—80, 90, 100 years old—yet if one partakes of sensuality, lives in the midst of sensuality, burns with sensual fever, is chewed up by sensual thoughts, and is eager in the search for sensuality,
an2.38:2.3: then one is reckoned simply as a young fool, not an elder.
an2.38:2.4: “But if one is a youngster, youthful—a black-haired young person endowed with the blessings of youth in the first stage of life—
an2.38:2.5: yet does not partake of sensuality, does not live in the midst of sensuality, does not burn with sensual fever, is not chewed up by sensual thoughts, and is not eager in the search for sensuality,
an2.38:2.6: then one is reckoned as a wise elder.”
an2.38:3.1: When this was said, Kaṇḍarāyana the brahman rose up from his seat, arranged his cloak over one shoulder, and bowed down at the feet of the monks who were youngsters, (saying,)
an2.38:3.2: “You, sirs, are the venerable ones, standing on the level of those who are venerable.
an2.38:3.3: We are the youngsters, standing on the level of those who are youngsters.
an2.38:4.1: “Magnificent, Master Kaccāna! … May Master Kaccāna remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forward, for life.”
